title: Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)
learning_outcomes: Formulate the Encoder-Decoder framework and explain the role of the bottleneck context vector.
Formulate the Teacher Forcing training strategy and trace its exposure bias limitations during inference.
Compare Luong (multiplicative) and Bahdanau (additive) attention alignment calculations mathematically.

content_sections.title: The Encoder-Decoder Framework & Teacher Forcing
content_sections.content:
The **Sequence-to-Sequence (Seq2Seq)** framework maps a variable-length input sequence $\\mathbf{x}_1 \\dots \\mathbf{x}_{T_x}$ to a variable-length output sequence $\\mathbf{y}_1 \\dots \\mathbf{y}_{T_y}$ (e.g. machine translation or summarization).

### 1. The Bottleneck Problem
*   **Encoder:** An RNN processes the input sequence and summarizes it into a single fixed-size **context vector** $\\mathbf{v}$ (typically the final hidden state $\\mathbf{h}_{T_x}$).
*   **Decoder:** Another RNN takes the context vector $\\mathbf{v}$ as its initial hidden state $\\mathbf{s}_0$ and generates the output sequence token-by-token.
*   **The Bottleneck:** Compressing an entire sentence of arbitrary length into a single fixed-size vector $\\mathbf{v}$ creates an information bottleneck, leading to rapid degradation on long sequences.

### 2. Teacher Forcing Training Protocol
During training, the decoder generates tokens sequentially. If we feed the decoder's own predicted token $\\hat{\\mathbf{y}}_{t-1}$ as the input for step $t$, early errors will propagate, causing training to be slow and unstable.
*   **Teacher Forcing:** We bypass the decoder's predictions during training. Instead, we feed the **ground-truth target token** $\\mathbf{y}_{t-1}$ directly as the input for step $t$:

$$\\mathbf{s}_t = \\operatorname{RNNCell}(\\mathbf{y}_{t-1}, \\mathbf{s}_{t-1})$$

This keeps the training trajectory aligned, accelerating convergence.

callouts.title: Exposure Bias in Teacher Forcing
callouts.content: Teacher forcing creates a mismatch between training and inference. During training, the decoder always receives perfect ground-truth inputs. During inference, ground truth is unavailable, so the decoder must run autoregressively (feeding its own predictions $\\hat{\\mathbf{y}}_{t-1}$ as inputs). If it makes a single error, the state drifts into regions of parameter space it never encountered during training, causing cascading errors. This phenomenon is known as **exposure bias**.

content_sections.title: Recurrent Attention: Luong vs. Bahdanau Alignment
content_sections.content:
To resolve the bottleneck problem, Bahdanau et al. (2015) introduced the **Attention Mechanism**. Instead of compressing the input into a single vector $\\mathbf{v}$, the encoder outputs all hidden states $\\mathbf{h}_1 \\dots \\mathbf{h}_{T_x}$. The decoder dynamically selects which encoder states to focus on at each step.

Let $\\mathbf{s}_t$ be the current decoder hidden state, and $\\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}$ be the encoder hidden state at step $i$.

### 1. Luong (Multiplicative) Attention
Luong attention computes the alignment score between the current decoder state $\\mathbf{s}_t$ and each encoder state $\\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}$ using a matrix multiplication:

$$s_{\\text{Luong}}(\\mathbf{s}_t, \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}) = \\mathbf{s}_t^{\\top} \\mathbf{W}_a \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}$$

Where $\\mathbf{W}_a \\in \\mathbb{R}^{h \\times h}$ is a learnable parameter matrix.

### 2. Bahdanau (Additive) Attention
Bahdanau attention computes alignment scores using a feedforward layer with a tanh activation. It uses the *previous* decoder state $\\mathbf{s}_{t-1}$:

$$s_{\\text{Bahdanau}}(\\mathbf{s}_{t-1}, \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}) = \\mathbf{v}_a^{\\top} \\tanh(\\mathbf{W}_a \\mathbf{s}_{t-1} + \\mathbf{U}_a \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}})$$

Where $\\mathbf{W}_a$ and $\\mathbf{U}_a$ are weight matrices, and $\\mathbf{v}_a$ is a projection vector.

callouts.title: Attention Speed Bottlenecks
callouts.content: Luong Multiplicative Attention is faster and more memory-efficient than Bahdanau Additive Attention. It can be implemented as a single highly optimized matrix multiplication: $\\mathbf{S}^{\\top} \\mathbf{W}_a \\mathbf{H}$. Bahdanau attention requires intermediate addition and activation calculations, which consume more memory and slow down training on large vocabularies.

content_sections.title: Context Vector Aggregation & Projections
content_sections.content:
Once the alignment scores are calculated, we normalize them to get attention weights.

### 1. Softmax Normalization
We apply the softmax function to normalize the alignment scores into a probability distribution over the encoder steps:

$$\\alpha_{t, i} = \\frac{e^{s(\\mathbf{s}_t, \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}})}}{\\sum_{j=1}^{T_x} e^{s(\\mathbf{s}_t, \\mathbf{h}_j^{\\text{enc}})}}$$

Where $\\alpha_{t, i}$ is the attention weight reflecting the relevance of the $i$-th encoder token to the current decoder step $t$.

### 2. Context Vector Calculation
The context vector $\\mathbf{c}_t$ is computed as the weighted sum of all encoder hidden states:

$$\\mathbf{c}_t = \\sum_{i=1}^{T_x} \\alpha_{t, i} \\mathbf{h}_i^{\\text{enc}}$$

### 3. Attentional Hidden State
Finally, we concatenate the context vector $\\mathbf{c}_t$ and the current decoder hidden state $\\mathbf{s}_t$, and pass them through a linear layer and tanh activation to get the attentional hidden state vector $\\tilde{\\mathbf{s}}_t$:

$$\\tilde{\\mathbf{s}}_t = \\tanh(\\mathbf{W}_c [\\mathbf{c}_t ; \\mathbf{s}_t] + \\mathbf{b}_c)$$

This vector is then projected to the vocabulary classes to predict the output token.
